label: fiction
title: Locker Seventy-Two
setting: A parcel-locker possession trial in 2026, where one phone number, two neighbouring lockers, and a blocked camera angle leave identity disputed.
charge: Knowingly possessing stolen prescription medicine with intent to sell
elements: Ilya possessed or controlled the stolen medicine found in locker seventy-two.
He knew the medicine was stolen.
He intended to sell or distribute it.
hook: A locker opens from a text message. Police find stolen medicine inside. The phone number has belonged to two people in one month.

accused.human: Ilya Morn works rotating shifts and spends spare evenings translating forms for newly arrived neighbours.
accused.if_guilty: A conviction means prison, loss of housing tied to employment, and the collapse of informal support many neighbours rely upon.

prosecution.speaker: pc
prosecution.text: Stolen medicine was recovered from locker seventy-two. Its collection code reached the phone number Ilya controlled, a camera placed him at the locker bank when the door moved, and he later acknowledged seeing a related message. The prosecution says those facts show he came to collect stock he knew was stolen and meant to sell.

defence.speaker: dc
defence.text: Ilya was there to collect work boots from locker seventy-three. The defence says the service matched an older booking to whoever held the phone number weeks later, while a pillar hid which door he opened. Carrier dates and his own collection record will test whether a code and a crowded camera view identify a person beyond reasonable doubt.

prosecution.speaker: pc
prosecution.text: The code reached Ilya’s phone number, he stood beside locker seventy-two when its door moved, and he later found a message carrying the collection code. His work-boots explanation does not erase those facts. Ask whether the carrier and camera records truly break the connection, or whether the evidence together proves he controlled the stolen medicine and intended to collect it for sale.

defence.speaker: dc
defence.text: Put every event in order. Locker seventy-two was booked before the carrier assigned its phone number to Ilya. His boots order opened locker seventy-three while the camera’s pillar hid his hands. The only related message he saw arrived after police contacted him. The prosecution must prove possession, knowledge, and intent to sell; Ilya does not have to identify whoever made the earlier booking.

epilogue: During the months before trial, Ilya lost the housing attached to his job and neighbours divided the translation work he had handled. The locker operator changed its warrant process to preserve the subscriber date attached to each booking, not just the person holding a number when police searched. Investigators reopened the older delivery account linked to locker seventy-two. Ilya’s family kept the work-boots receipt with the court papers.

beats.speaker: pc
beats.text: Exhibit one is the sealed medicine recovered from locker seventy-two and the service record for its collection code. The record lists a mobile number, not a customer name. When police searched that number, the carrier’s current-subscriber return named Ilya. The locker service had not stored who held the number when the compartment was booked or who read the earlier reminders.
beats.reveal_note: The code looked personal because police searched the number after finding the medicine. The record itself contained no subscriber name or assignment date.

beats.speaker: w2
beats.text: I reviewed the hall camera. Ilya enters at 6:18 and walks toward lockers seventy-two and seventy-three. A pillar hides his hands for twelve seconds. Locker seventy-two’s door moves just afterwards, but the footage does not show who opened it. My first report linked the movement to Ilya because he was the clearest person in frame; no other face could be identified.
beats.reveal_note: The timing placed Ilya near the compartment, but the blocked view could not establish which door he touched.

beats.speaker: w1
beats.text: When you arrested Ilya, had you obtained the number’s assignment history? No. We had a current-subscriber return. Did it identify the subscriber when locker seventy-two was booked? No. It showed only who held the number when we made the request. We obtained the historical dates after the arrest.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: When you arrested Ilya, had you obtained the number’s assignment history?

turns.speaker: w1
turns.text: No. We had a current-subscriber return.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: Did it identify the subscriber when locker seventy-two was booked?

turns.speaker: w1
turns.text: No. It showed only who held the number when we made the request. We obtained the historical dates after the arrest.

beats.reveal_note: Police had identified the subscriber on the search date, not on the earlier booking date.

beats.speaker: pc
beats.text: Exhibit four is the final reminder for locker seventy-two. It was sent after Ilya had taken over the phone number, and his handset marked it delivered. The message did not name the medicine or the person who booked the compartment, but it carried the same collection code as the earlier reminders. Police say it gave Ilya the means to open the door.
beats.reveal_note: The final reminder gave Ilya access to the code, but it did not show that he made the booking or ever used it.

beats.speaker: w3
beats.text: I certified the carrier’s assignment history. It shows the locker number moving to Ilya six days after locker seventy-two was booked and two days after the first collection reminder. The earlier subscriber’s name is withheld, but the dates come from our billing system, were recorded before this investigation began, and were not edited by police.
beats.reveal_note: The certified dates showed that Ilya did not hold the number when the medicine compartment was booked or first announced.

beats.speaker: w2
beats.text: I checked the second camera and the neighbouring compartment log. The parcel in Ilya’s hands carries the order number assigned to locker seventy-three. That door opened at 6:18 using a code sent to his email address. The records cannot show every movement behind the pillar, but they confirm he had a separate parcel to collect there.
beats.reveal_note: The second camera and order log independently supported Ilya’s stated reason for being at the locker bank.

beats.speaker: w4
beats.text: I ordered work boots for my next shift. The email told me to use locker seventy-three, and that is the door I opened. I did not know medicine was in the next compartment. The first locker-seventy-two message I saw was already in my phone after police contacted me and asked me to search for it.
beats.reveal_note: Ilya’s account matched his email receipt and the neighbouring locker record, although his testimony still required the jury’s assessment.

beats.speaker: w2
beats.text: The police search used the number as it existed that day? Yes. It returned the current subscriber. So the result joined Ilya to an earlier booking, without warning police about the date mismatch? Yes. It displayed one current customer result with no historical warning.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: The police search used the number as it existed that day?

turns.speaker: w2
turns.text: Yes. It returned the current subscriber.

turns.speaker: dc
turns.text: So the result joined Ilya to an earlier booking, without warning police about the date mismatch?

turns.speaker: w2
turns.text: Yes. It displayed one current customer result with no historical warning.

beats.reveal_note: The search interface presented a current subscriber as though that person belonged to the earlier booking.

beats.speaker: w4
beats.text: You did receive the collection code on your phone? I found one message after police contacted me and asked me to search. You never tried that code at locker seventy-two? No. I had already used my email code at locker seventy-three. I did not open the medicine compartment or know what was inside it.

turns.speaker: pc
turns.text: You did receive the collection code on your phone?

turns.speaker: w4
turns.text: I found one message after police contacted me and asked me to search.

turns.speaker: pc
turns.text: You never tried that code at locker seventy-two?

turns.speaker: w4
turns.text: No. I had already used my email code at locker seventy-three. I did not open the medicine compartment or know what was inside it.

beats.reveal_note: The late reminder showed that Ilya eventually received the code, but not that he possessed the medicine or knew why the locker existed.

beats.speaker: judge
beats.text: Do not research this case or the locker service. The prosecution must make you sure Ilya controlled the medicine, knew it was stolen, and intended to sell or distribute it. His presence or receipt of a late code is not enough if the records leave a reasonable possibility that the earlier booking belonged to someone else.
beats.reveal_note: The legal test required proof of possession, knowledge, and intent to sell; Ilya did not have to identify the earlier subscriber.

twist: The number on the locker record did belong to Ilya by the time police searched it. It did not belong to him when the compartment was booked. A second camera and the collection log placed him at locker seventy-three for his own parcel, turning a seemingly personal code into a database timing error.

jurors.label: Juror 2
jurors.persona: An empathetic juror who notices harm but refuses to let sympathy replace proof. In Locker Seventy-Two, this juror keeps returning to the locker code associated with Ilya’s current phone number.

agree: Juror 2: The delivered code and Ilya’s presence still need an explanation. I am not dismissing them.
Juror 2: A code on his phone matters, but only if the dates connect him to the booking.
pushback: Juror 2: I want the booking date, the subscriber date, and the door movement kept separate.
Juror 2: Ilya’s explanation need only be reasonably possible. It does not have to be perfect.
concede: Juror 2: The number changed hands after the booking. That weakens the clue I trusted first.
final: Juror 2: I am weighing his presence and late code against the earlier booking and the locker-seventy-three record.

jurors.label: Juror 3
jurors.persona: A systems-minded juror alert to both automation errors and deliberate manipulation. In Locker Seventy-Two, this juror keeps returning to the locker code associated with Ilya’s current phone number.

agree: Juror 3: The carrier dates and locker-seventy-three log are independent records. Together they support Ilya’s account.
Juror 3: His boots receipt can be tested against the camera and door log. It is not just his word.
pushback: Juror 3: Which record proves Ilya controlled the medicine, rather than merely standing beside it?
Juror 3: I am following the timestamps, not choosing the tidier story.
concede: Juror 3: The current-subscriber result cannot answer who held the number on the booking date.
final: Juror 3: I need the booking, phone assignment, and door records to form one chronology before I convict.

jurors.label: Juror 4
jurors.persona: A quiet questioner who tests what the strongest theory still leaves unexplained. In Locker Seventy-Two, this juror keeps returning to the locker code associated with Ilya’s current phone number.

agree: Juror 4: The timing is evidence, but it does not replace proof of possession, knowledge, and intent.
pushback: Juror 4: A code received later does not by itself prove who made the earlier booking.
Juror 4: I will not turn Ilya’s presence beside two lockers into control of both.
concede: Juror 4: The assignment history exposes a real gap in the prosecution’s identification.
holdout: Juror 4: Show me evidence that Ilya controlled locker seventy-two, not evidence that he stood nearby.
Juror 4: The prosecution carries the burden on every element. Ilya carries none.
final: Juror 4: My verdict turns on whether the prosecution proved control of the medicine, not mere opportunity.

jurors.label: Juror 5
jurors.persona: A steady foreperson who makes the room distinguish suspicion from proof. In Locker Seventy-Two, this juror keeps returning to the locker code associated with Ilya’s current phone number.

agree: Juror 5: The boots order, second camera, and carrier history give the defence account a factual foundation.
pushback: Juror 5: I am separating access to a late code from knowing control of stolen medicine.
Juror 5: I need the witness account and the records to agree before I rely on either.
concede: Juror 5: The assignment date is independently recorded. That is more than a convenient explanation.
burden_correct: Juror 5: Unanswered questions do not shift the burden. The prosecution must prove every element.
Juror 5: Ilya does not have to identify the earlier subscriber for reasonable doubt to exist.
final: Juror 5: The question is whether the evidence proves possession and knowledge, not whether Ilya can solve the case.

jurors.label: Juror 6
jurors.persona: A careful note-taker who separates a striking clue from the chain needed to prove identity. In Locker Seventy-Two, this juror keeps returning to the locker code associated with Ilya’s current phone number.

agree: Juror 6: The late code and Ilya’s presence are suspicious, but the booking date may change their meaning.
Juror 6: The boots record explains why he was there. We still have to test whether it explains everything.
pushback: Juror 6: Let us identify which door the evidence actually shows Ilya opening.
Juror 6: We should not count the phone lookup and locker code twice when both depend on one number.
concede: Juror 6: Once the dates are aligned, the current-subscriber search loses much of its force.
final: Juror 6: The defence account need only remain reasonably possible, not answer every loose end.

jurors.label: Juror 7
jurors.persona: A practical juror who tests digital records against ordinary workplace habits. In Locker Seventy-Two, this juror keeps returning to the locker code associated with Ilya’s current phone number.

agree: Juror 7: Ilya’s own account still has to explain the collection code that reached his phone.
Juror 7: If his testimony conflicts with the delivery records, that would move me toward guilt.
pushback: Juror 7: I keep coming back to the code on Ilya’s phone. How much does its late arrival matter?
Juror 7: The blocked camera still leaves room for either account. I am not ready to settle.
concede: Juror 7: The locker system joined two different moments. I had treated them as one.
Juror 7: I accept that standing beside locker seventy-two is not the same as opening it.
final: Juror 7: Confidence cannot repair the missing link between Ilya and the original booking.

jurors.label: Juror 8
jurors.persona: A sceptic who distrusts polished system evidence until its collection method is clear. In Locker Seventy-Two, this juror keeps returning to the locker code associated with Ilya’s current phone number.

agree: Juror 8: The booking predates Ilya’s number assignment, and locker seventy-three opened for his real order.
Juror 8: Those two independent timestamps move me more than the current-customer label.
pushback: Juror 8: The code feels decisive because it is personal. The assignment history says it may not be.
Juror 8: I will not count one database join as both identity and possession.
concede: Juror 8: The timeline changes how much weight I give the code, but I am still testing it.
final: Juror 8: The dated records changed my mind; they explain both the phone number and Ilya’s presence.

jurors.label: Juror 9
jurors.persona: A rule-focused juror who brings the room back to the prosecution burden. In Locker Seventy-Two, this juror keeps returning to the locker code associated with Ilya’s current phone number.

agree: Juror 9: The carrier history and neighbouring-locker log expose how the first identification was made.
Juror 9: I am weighing the dated records beside the late code, not pretending either stands alone.
pushback: Juror 9: The police search was a reasonable lead, but a lead is not proof.
Juror 9: I am not filling the missing step between nearby and in control with instinct.
concede: Juror 9: I would rather name the gap in the subscriber record than pretend it is not there.
final: Juror 9: The chronology must show possession, knowledge, and intent. At present it shows suspicion.

jurors.label: Juror 10
jurors.persona: A patient listener who changes position when a timeline finally accounts for every event. In Locker Seventy-Two, this juror keeps returning to the locker code associated with Ilya’s current phone number.

agree: Juror 10: The late code and blocked camera still leave questions I want answered.
pushback: Juror 10: The subscriber mismatch may be material, but I am not sure it explains every message.
burden_drift: Juror 10: I keep wanting Ilya to tell us who booked locker seventy-two.
Juror 10: If Ilya cannot explain the earlier subscriber, part of me wants to hold that against him.
concede: Juror 10: The dated records explain more of the sequence than the current-customer label does.
burden_correct: Juror 10: Ilya does not have to identify the earlier subscriber. The prosecution keeps the burden.
final: Juror 10: My unanswered questions cannot substitute for proof of possession, knowledge, and intent.

jurors.label: Juror 11
jurors.persona: A blunt juror who challenges convenient memory from witnesses on both sides. In Locker Seventy-Two, this juror keeps returning to the locker code associated with Ilya’s current phone number.

agree: Juror 11: The carrier and locker records are the facts I can test rather than guess.
Juror 11: Put the code on the same timeline as the booking and assignment dates. Then its limits are clear.
pushback: Juror 11: I have not decided what the late code proves about Ilya’s intentions.
Juror 11: The camera shows proximity. I still need evidence of control.
concede: Juror 11: I am ready to put less weight on the current-subscriber result.
final: Juror 11: The dated records create reasonable doubt about who booked and controlled locker seventy-two.

jurors.label: Juror 12
jurors.persona: A cautious holdout who wants the decisive record stated without exaggeration. In Locker Seventy-Two, this juror keeps returning to the locker code associated with Ilya’s current phone number.

agree: Juror 12: The boots record explains Ilya’s presence without requiring us to invent another fact.
Juror 12: The assignment history changes the code from an identity record into a timing question.
pushback: Juror 12: I hear the point about the code. Now I want its source and timing tested.
Juror 12: Strip away the customer label. What fact connects Ilya to the original booking?
concede: Juror 12: The current-subscriber result cannot carry the identification by itself.
final: Juror 12: The defence has a supported account, while the prosecution’s chain still lacks control and knowledge.

gen_meta.reviewer: Codex Content Curator; independent QA rewrite and dynamics verification
